_meta.title: AP Bio mid-grained misconception taxonomy (litmus draft)
_meta.status: AUTHORED / ILLUSTRATIVE DRAFT — not expert-reviewed. See docs/litmus_plan.md.
_meta.purpose: Mid-grained layer for the SLM-pivot litmus test. Each misconception is scoped to a few AP Bio topics and MAPS UP to one coarse cognitive-kind label from common_bio.py (content_gap | reasoning_error | misread_or_passage_mapping). This is the two-layer relationship: the SLM tags the mid-grained misconception; that misconception deterministically rolls up to the coarse cognitive-kind that feeds the three-score product.
_meta.granularity_note: This sits between the 5-ish coarse cognitive-kind labels (too coarse; a prompt may already do them) and a 2,500-way Eedi-style retrieval bank (too fine; that is re-running the retrieval benchmark). Target size ~30-50.
_meta.coarse_source: scripts/common_bio.py LABELS (v1 taxonomy, minus abstain which is a meta/decision label, not a misconception)
_meta.generalization_target: IN-DISTRIBUTION misconceptions on NOVEL item stems. Held-out eval items reuse these same misconceptions on questions the model never saw tagged; we do NOT ask the model to invent unseen misconceptions.

misconceptions.name: Individuals adapt within a lifetime
misconceptions.description: Believes an individual organism evolves or acquires adaptations during its own life in response to need, rather than allele frequencies shifting across generations (Lamarckian).

misconceptions.name: Need directs mutation
misconceptions.description: Thinks organisms produce the specific mutations they 'need'; treats mutation as goal-directed rather than random with respect to fitness.

misconceptions.name: Use/disuse traits are inherited
misconceptions.description: Assumes traits strengthened or lost through use/disuse are passed to offspring (soft inheritance).

misconceptions.name: Evolution progresses toward perfection
misconceptions.description: Treats evolution as a directed ladder toward more complex or 'better' organisms rather than differential reproductive success in a context.

misconceptions.name: Fitness means physical strength
misconceptions.description: Equates fitness with being strongest/fastest instead of relative reproductive success in a given environment.

misconceptions.name: Traits evolve for the good of the species
misconceptions.description: Reasons that a trait evolved to benefit the species/group rather than because it raised individual reproductive success.

misconceptions.name: Selection creates variation
misconceptions.description: Thinks natural selection generates new variation, rather than acting on pre-existing heritable variation produced by mutation/recombination.

misconceptions.name: Dominant allele is more common
misconceptions.description: Assumes a dominant allele must be more frequent in the population than the recessive one.

misconceptions.name: Masked recessive allele disappears
misconceptions.description: Believes a recessive allele is destroyed or lost when masked by a dominant allele, rather than still present and heritable.

misconceptions.name: Alleles blend
misconceptions.description: Treats inheritance as blending of parental traits rather than discrete alleles that segregate.

misconceptions.name: Applies the wrong Punnett ratio
misconceptions.description: Knows the 9:3:3:1 / 3:1 / 1:16 ratios but maps the wrong fraction onto the requested phenotype combination.

misconceptions.name: Confuses genotype and phenotype
misconceptions.description: Reports a genotype ratio when asked for a phenotype ratio (or vice versa).

misconceptions.name: Ignores independent assortment
misconceptions.description: Fails to treat two genes as assorting independently; multiplies or combines probabilities incorrectly for dihybrid outcomes.

misconceptions.name: Confuses homozygous and heterozygous
misconceptions.description: Mislabels or miscounts homozygous vs heterozygous genotypes when reasoning about a cross.

misconceptions.name: Ignores sex linkage
misconceptions.description: Treats an X-linked trait as autosomal, ignoring that inheritance differs by sex.

misconceptions.name: O2 donates electrons
misconceptions.description: Reverses O2's role, treating it as an electron donor rather than the terminal (final) electron acceptor of the ETC.

misconceptions.name: Fermentation directly makes ATP
misconceptions.description: Believes fermentation itself generates ATP, rather than regenerating NAD+ so glycolysis can continue producing ATP.

misconceptions.name: ETC runs without oxygen
misconceptions.description: Thinks the electron transport chain operates under anaerobic conditions (no terminal acceptor needed).

misconceptions.name: Glycolysis requires oxygen
misconceptions.description: Assumes glycolysis is aerobic and cannot proceed without O2.

misconceptions.name: O2 phosphorylates ADP directly
misconceptions.description: Conflates O2's terminal-acceptor role with ATP synthase's phosphorylation step; attributes ATP formation directly to O2.

misconceptions.name: Reverses lactate/pyruvate in fermentation
misconceptions.description: States fermentation breaks lactate back into pyruvate to regenerate NAD+, reversing the actual pyruvate-to-lactate step.

misconceptions.name: NADH is ATP
misconceptions.description: Treats NADH as a direct ATP product rather than an electron carrier that feeds the ETC.

misconceptions.name: Plants only photosynthesize
misconceptions.description: Believes plants photosynthesize instead of respiring, not that they do both (respiration continues day and night).

misconceptions.name: Released O2 comes from CO2
misconceptions.description: Thinks the O2 released in photosynthesis is split from CO2 rather than from water (photolysis).

misconceptions.name: Calvin cycle needs light directly
misconceptions.description: Assumes the Calvin cycle requires light directly, rather than the ATP/NADPH produced by the light reactions.

misconceptions.name: Swaps light and Calvin reactions
misconceptions.description: Attributes products/inputs of the light reactions to the Calvin cycle or vice versa.

misconceptions.name: Reverses osmosis direction
misconceptions.description: Predicts water moving toward the lower solute concentration; reverses which way water moves relative to tonicity.

misconceptions.name: Solute moves in osmosis
misconceptions.description: Expects solute to cross the membrane to balance concentrations instead of water moving (ignores selective permeability).

misconceptions.name: Swaps hypertonic and hypotonic
misconceptions.description: Reverses the definitions of hypertonic vs hypotonic when predicting cell behavior.

misconceptions.name: Confuses active and passive transport
misconceptions.description: Mislabels a transport process as requiring or not requiring ATP (e.g., calls facilitated diffusion active).

misconceptions.name: Diffusion requires energy
misconceptions.description: Thinks movement down a concentration gradient requires cellular energy input.

misconceptions.name: Molecules stop at equilibrium
misconceptions.description: Assumes molecular movement ceases at equilibrium rather than net movement being zero while individuals keep moving.

misconceptions.name: Assumes equilibrium instead of reasoning about the gradient
misconceptions.description: Concludes 'no net movement' without reasoning that a concentration difference still exists.

misconceptions.name: Enzymes are used up
misconceptions.description: Believes enzymes are consumed by the reactions they catalyze rather than reused.

misconceptions.name: Enzymes raise activation energy
misconceptions.description: States enzymes increase activation energy, reversing their role of lowering it.

misconceptions.name: Enzymes change the equilibrium
misconceptions.description: Thinks enzymes shift the reaction equilibrium or change ΔG rather than only speeding the approach to equilibrium.

misconceptions.name: Higher temperature always speeds enzymes
misconceptions.description: Reasons that raising temperature always increases enzyme rate, ignoring denaturation past the optimum.

misconceptions.name: Confuses competitive and noncompetitive inhibition
misconceptions.description: Mixes up whether an inhibitor binds the active site and whether added substrate can overcome it.

misconceptions.name: Enzymes act on any substrate
misconceptions.description: Ignores active-site specificity, treating an enzyme as able to catalyze unrelated reactions.

misconceptions.name: pH does not affect enzymes
misconceptions.description: Assumes pH is irrelevant to enzyme activity, ignoring optimal-pH effects on structure.

misconceptions.name: Answers a single trait for a joint question
misconceptions.description: Gives the probability/fraction for one trait when the stem asks for the joint (both-traits, 'AND') outcome.

misconceptions.name: Calls a clear trend random error
misconceptions.description: Labels a systematic, ordered pattern in the data as random measurement error, ignoring the described trend.

misconceptions.name: Confuses variable roles or control
misconceptions.description: Mixes up independent vs dependent variable, or misidentifies the control group, when interpreting an experiment.

misconceptions.name: Misses EXCEPT/NOT qualifier
misconceptions.description: Answers as if the stem asked the opposite because a negation qualifier (EXCEPT, NOT, LEAST) was overlooked.

misconceptions.name: Misreads a figure or axis
misconceptions.description: Draws a conclusion inconsistent with the graph/axis actually shown (misreads scale, axis, or which curve).

misconceptions.name: Attributes mass change to solute movement
misconceptions.description: Interprets a dialysis/osmosis mass change as solute diffusion rather than water movement described by the data.
